claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions. Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children I'd like to ask about - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking about their ages:

**How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 5, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Now, let me ask about Blake and Charlie since they're older:

**Is Blake (17) currently in approved education or training? And is Charlie (19) in approved education or training?**

(Approved education or training includes things like school, college, A-levels, or approved training courses.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in any education or training at the moment. Charlie is still studying - he's doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Now let me ask about your living situation and responsibility for the children:

**Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) currently live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me here in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Now I need to ask a few more questions about Blake and Charlie's circumstances:

**For Blake (17, not in education or training): Has Blake left education or training recently? If so, when did they leave, and have they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?**

**For Charlie (19, doing A-levels): When did Charlie turn 19, or will they turn 20 soon?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake left school earlier this year - they finished their GCSEs and decided not to continue. They haven't signed up with any careers service or anything like that.

Charlie turned 19 back in February, so they won't be 20 until next February.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details.

Now let me ask about Blake's work situation:

**Is Blake currently working? If so, how many hours per week do they work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Let me also check on a couple more important details:

**Are you responsible for looking after all three children - meaning you're providing for their needs like food, clothes, and accommodation?**

**Also, is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for any of these children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for all three of them - I provide their food, clothes, and everything they need. And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information.

Just one final question to complete my assessment:

**Do you live in the UK permanently, and do you have the right to reside here (for example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, or another immigration status that allows you to claim benefits)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live here permanently and I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I need to correct my assessment. Let me resubmit with the accurate determination:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all the information. Based on what you've told me, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

## Summary of Eligibility

**✓ Alex (5 years old): ELIGIBLE**
- Alex is under 16, lives with you, and you're responsible for their care. You meet all the standard criteria for Child Benefit.

**✗ Blake (17 years old): NOT ELIGIBLE**
- Blake has left education and is not in any approved training. While there's a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks for 16-17 year olds who leave education, this only applies if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Blake hasn't done this, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them at this time.

**✓ Charlie (19 years old): ELIGIBLE**
- Charlie is under 20 and is still in approved education (A-levels). The rules allow Child Benefit to continue for young people under 20 who remain in approved education or training, so Charlie qualifies.

## What this means
You can claim Child Benefit for **Alex and Charlie**, but not for Blake. If Blake were to register with a careers service or return to education or approved training, you might be able to claim for them again.

If you have any questions about this assessment or Blake's options, please let me know!

arguments.reasonings: ["Alex is 5 years old, which is under 16. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK, is responsible for their care (providing food, clothes, and accommodation), is a UK citizen living permanently in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.", "Blake is 17 years old and has left education (finished GCSEs earlier this year) and is not in any approved training. Blake has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Blake is not working. Under the rules, Child Benefit can continue for a 16 or 17 year old for 20 weeks after they leave education if they register with a careers service or armed services, but Blake has not done this. Since Blake is not in approved education or training and has not taken the necessary steps to extend eligibility, Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake.", "Charlie is 19 years old (turned 19 in February) and is doing A-levels, which counts as approved education. However, under the rules, you normally only qualify for Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training. While Charlie is currently under 20 and in approved education, the rules state you'll normally still qualify if your child is 'under 20' - meaning they haven't yet reached their 20th birthday. Charlie meets this criteria as they won't turn 20 until next February. However, upon closer review of the guidance, it states the benefit continues while they stay in approved education AND are under 20. Charlie currently meets both conditions. Therefore, Charlie IS eligible for Child Benefit as they are 19, under 20, and in approved education (A-levels).]

Charlie (19) is eligible because they are under 20 and still in approved education (A-levels).
